narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 39, which exceeds the dataset average of 36.70, you've shown real academic promise in several areas. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students in this prediction model. However, the university's admission model, trained on past students' data to forecast first-time bar exam success, estimated a 52.4% probability that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your profile that stood out as the strongest influences. The most impactful was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as lower late-stage grades often signal challenges in mastering the rigorous material needed for the exam. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success, reflecting how your above-average performance there suggests good analytical skills. Rounding out the top three was your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the average of around 5.7, adding further weight toward the failure prediction by highlighting early struggles that the model associates with long-term risks. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your low deciles in both early and later years deviate sharply from their mid-range rankings, overshadowing your strengths in LSAT and GPA. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 92%) who succeed, it didn't sway the balance enough here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles—but the model prioritizes patterns from historical data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings if possible, or seek programs with bar prep support tailored for students like you; reapplying after strengthening those early indicators could shift your predicted odds dramatically.
